output.title: Landlord Withholding Your Security Deposit? Here’s What to Do Next
output.meta_description: Is your landlord withholding your security deposit? Learn your tenant rights, what landlords can deduct, and how to demand your deposit back fast.
content_outline: The Frustration: When Your Landlord Won't Give You Your Deposit Back
Can a Landlord Keep Your Security Deposit? Know the Legal Limits
What Can a Landlord Deduct From Your Security Deposit (and What’s Off-Limits)
State-by-State Deadlines: How Long a Landlord Has to Return Your Deposit
What to Do When Your Landlord Doesn't Return Your Deposit: A 3-Step Action Plan
How to Write a Security Deposit Dispute Letter That Actually Works
Escalation Tactics: From Demand Letters to Small Claims Court
Final Checklist: Recover Your Deposit Before Time Runs Out
key_points: Landlords can only deduct for actual damage beyond normal wear and tear or unpaid rent; they cannot withhold deposits arbitrarily.
Most states require landlords to return security deposits within 14 to 60 days and provide an itemized deduction list.
A professionally written security deposit dispute letter is the most effective first step to recovery and often avoids small claims court.
Tenants in many states may recover double or triple damages, plus attorney fees, if a landlord wrongfully withholds a deposit.
output.cta: Don’t let your landlord keep what’s yours. Create your state-specific security deposit demand letter with DepositBack in minutes and start your recovery today.
output.target_audience: Frustrated US tenants dealing with a landlord withholding, delaying, or refusing to return a security deposit, who need immediate, legally sound recovery steps.
